subject: Physics
q: A body is at rest at $x=0$. At $t=0$, it starts moving in the positive $x$-direction with a constant acceleration. At the same instant another body passes through $x=0$ moving in the positive $x$ direction with a constant speed. The position of the first body is given by $\mathrm{x}_1(\mathrm{t})$ after time ' $\mathrm{t}$ ' and that of the second body by $x_2(t)$ after the same time interval. Which of the following graphs correctly describes $\left(x_1-x_2\right)$ as a function of time ' $t$ '?
question: A body is at rest at $x=0$. At $t=0$, it starts moving in the positive $x$-direction with a constant acceleration. At the same instant another body passes through $x=0$ moving in the positive $x$ direction with a constant speed. The position of the first body is given by $\mathrm{x}_1(\mathrm{t})$ after time ' $\mathrm{t}$ ' and that of the second body by $x_2(t)$ after the same time interval. Which of the following graphs correctly describes $\left(x_1-x_2\right)$ as a function of time ' $t$ '?
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& x_1(t)=\frac{1}{2} a t^2 \\
& x_2(t)=v t \\
& x_1-x_2=\frac{1}{2} a t^2-v t
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: A body travels $102.5 \mathrm{~m}$ in $\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$ second and $115.0 \mathrm{~m}$ in $(\mathrm{n}+2)^{\text {th }}$ second. The acceleration is :
question: A body travels $102.5 \mathrm{~m}$ in $\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$ second and $115.0 \mathrm{~m}$ in $(\mathrm{n}+2)^{\text {th }}$ second. The acceleration is :
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { Given, } 102.5=\mathrm{u}+\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2}(2 \mathrm{n}-1) \& \\
& 115=\mathrm{u}+\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2}(2 \mathrm{n}+3) \\
& \Rightarrow 102.5=\mathrm{u}+\mathrm{an}-\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2} \& \\
& 115=\mathrm{u}+\mathrm{an}+\frac{3 \mathrm{a}}{2} \\
& 12.5=2 \mathrm{a} \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=6.25 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A ball is released from a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> be the time required to complete first half and second half of the distance respectively. Then, choose the correct relation between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>.
question: A ball is released from a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> be the time required to complete first half and second half of the distance respectively. Then, choose the correct relation between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>.
solution:
As the body is dropped from a certain height h, its initial velocity will be zero.

For first half, h1=h2.

Using second equation of motion, s=ut+12at2

We have, h2=12gt12.

Time taken in travel first half of the height, t1=hg    ...1.

For total height h, using again second equation of motion,

h=12gt1+t22⇒t1+t2=2hg    ...2

From equation 1 and 2, 

⇒12=t1t1+t2

⇒1+t2t1=2

⇒t1t2=12-1

Thus, the relation is t2=2-1t1.

subject: Physics
q: A ball is thrown from a point with a speed $v_0$ at an angle of projection $\theta$. From the same point and at the same instant person starts running with a constant speed $v_0 / 2$ to catch the ball. Will the person be able to catch the ball? If yes, what should be the angle of projection?
question: A ball is thrown from a point with a speed $v_0$ at an angle of projection $\theta$. From the same point and at the same instant person starts running with a constant speed $v_0 / 2$ to catch the ball. Will the person be able to catch the ball? If yes, what should be the angle of projection?
solution:
$\mathrm{v}_0 \cos \theta=\frac{\mathrm{v}_0}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \theta=60^{\circ}$

subject: Physics
q:
A paratrooper jumps from an aeroplane and opens a parachute after 2 s of free fall and starts deaccelerating with $3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$. At 10 m height from ground, while descending with the help of parachute, the speed of paratrooper is $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The initial height of the airplane is $\_\_\_\_$ m.
$\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right)$
question:
A paratrooper jumps from an aeroplane and opens a parachute after 2 s of free fall and starts deaccelerating with $3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$. At 10 m height from ground, while descending with the help of parachute, the speed of paratrooper is $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The initial height of the airplane is $\_\_\_\_$ m.
$\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right)$
solution:
During the first $2\text{ s}$ of free fall, the paratrooper starts from rest ($u = 0$).
Velocity after $2\text{ s}$ is $v_1 = u + gt = 0 + 10 \times 2 = 20\text{ m/s}$.
Distance covered during free fall is $h_1 = \frac{1}{2}gt^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 2^2 = 20\text{ m}$.
After opening the parachute, the paratrooper decelerates at $a = -3\text{ m/s}^2$.
At a height of $10\text{ m}$ from the ground, the final velocity is $v_2 = 5\text{ m/s}$.
Let $h_2$ be the distance covered during deceleration until reaching $10\text{ m}$ height.
Using $v_2^2 = v_1^2 + 2ah_2$:
$5^2 = 20^2 + 2(-3)h_2$
$25 = 400 - 6h_2$
$6h_2 = 375$
$h_2 = 62.5\text{ m}$.
The total height of the airplane is the sum of the free fall distance, the deceleration distance, and the remaining height from the ground.
$H = h_1 + h_2 + 10 = 20 + 62.5 + 10 = 92.5\text{ m}$.

subject: Physics
q:
An object, moving with a speed of $6.25 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, is decelerated at a rate given by :
$$
\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}}=-2.5 \sqrt{\mathrm{v}}
$$
where $v$ is the instantaneous speed. The time taken by the object, to come to rest, would be:
question:
An object, moving with a speed of $6.25 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, is decelerated at a rate given by :
$$
\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}}=-2.5 \sqrt{\mathrm{v}}
$$
where $v$ is the instantaneous speed. The time taken by the object, to come to rest, would be:
solution:
$$
\frac{d v}{d t}=-2.5 \sqrt{v}
$$
Integrating the above equation.
$$
\Rightarrow 2 \sqrt{v}=-2.5 \mathrm{t}+\mathrm{C}
$$
at $\mathrm{t}=0, \mathrm{v}=6.25 \Rightarrow \mathrm{C}=5$
at $v=0 \Rightarrow t=\frac{5}{2.5}=2 \mathrm{~s}$

subject: Physics
q: A ball is thrown up vertically with a certain velocity so that, it reaches a maximum height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math>. Find the ratio of the times in which it is at height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>h</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> while going up and coming down respectively.
question: A ball is thrown up vertically with a certain velocity so that, it reaches a maximum height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math>. Find the ratio of the times in which it is at height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>h</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> while going up and coming down respectively.
solution:
The maximum height of a projectile thrown vertically upward is given by, h=u22g.

⇒u=2gh

Using equation of motion with constant acceleration,

s=ut+12at2

⇒h3=2ght-12gt2

⇒gt22-2ght+h3=0

Solving the above quadratic equation we will get the times t1 and t2. ⇒t2t1=2gh+2gh-4×g2×h32gh-2gh-4×g2×h3=2gh+4gh32gh-4gh3=3+23-2

Thus, the ratio t1t2=3-23+2

subject: Physics
q: Train A is moving along two parallel rail tracks towards north with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>72</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and train <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is moving towards south with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>108</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Velocity of train <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> with respect to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and velocity of ground with respect to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> are (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>):
question: Train A is moving along two parallel rail tracks towards north with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>72</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and train <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is moving towards south with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>108</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Velocity of train <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> with respect to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and velocity of ground with respect to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> are (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>):
solution:
VA=72 km h-1=72×10003600=20 m s-1 and 

VB=-108 km h-1=-108×10003600=-30 m s-1

Velocity of B w.r.t. A:

VB/A=-30-20=– 50 m s-1

Velocity of ground w.r.t. B:

VG/B=0--30=30 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: From a building two balls $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ are thrown such that $\mathrm{A}$ is thrown upwards $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ downwards (both vertically). If $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{B}}$ are their respective velocities on reaching the ground, then
question: From a building two balls $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ are thrown such that $\mathrm{A}$ is thrown upwards $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ downwards (both vertically). If $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{B}}$ are their respective velocities on reaching the ground, then
options: their velocities depend on their masses
solution:
As the ball moves down from height ' $h$ ' to ground the P.E at height ' $h$ ' is converted to K.E. at the ground (Applying Law of conservation of Energy)
Hence, $\frac{1}{2} m_A v_A^2=m_A g h_A$ or $v_A=\sqrt{2 g h_A}$; Similarly, $v_B=\sqrt{2 g h}$ or $v_A=v_B$

subject: Physics
q: From a tower of height H, a particle is thrown vertically upwards with a speed u. The time taken by the particle, to hit the ground, is n times that taken by it to reach the highest point of its path.The relation between H, u and n is :
question: From a tower of height H, a particle is thrown vertically upwards with a speed u. The time taken by the particle, to hit the ground, is n times that taken by it to reach the highest point of its path.The relation between H, u and n is :
solution:
nt AB = t AB + t BC + t CD

n - 1 t AB = t BC + t CD         ....(1)

n - 1 u g = u g + t CD

∴   n - 2 u g = t CD        ...(2)

H + h = 1 2 gt BD 2

H + u 2 2 g = 1 2 g n - 1 u g 2    ...from (1)

∴    H + u 2 2 g = n - 1 2 u 2 2 g

∴     H = u 2 2 g n - 1 2 - 1

2g H = nu2(n - 2)

Let $\alpha$ be the angle between their directions of motion. The relative velocity of the second particle w.r.t. the first is least after a time.

Let $\alpha$ be the angle between their directions of motion. The relative velocity of the second particle w.r.t. the first is least after a time.

subject: Physics
q: A particle initially at rest starts moving from reference point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis, with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math> that varies as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The acceleration of the particle is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
question: A particle initially at rest starts moving from reference point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis, with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math> that varies as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The acceleration of the particle is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution:
Given: v=4x

Instantaneous acceleration of the particle can be written as, a=vdvdx

=4x×4×12x-1/2=8 m s-2

subject: Physics
q: The position of a particle related to time is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>(</mo><mn>5</mn><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The magnitude of velocity of the particle at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> s will be :
question: The position of a particle related to time is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>(</mo><mn>5</mn><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The magnitude of velocity of the particle at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> s will be :
solution:
It is given that

x=5t2-4t+5

The velocity is given by 

v=dxdt=d(5t2-4t+5)dt=10t -4 

At t=2 s,

v=10(2)-4=16 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: A body moves on a frictionless plane starting from rest. If $S_n$ is distance moved between $t=n-1$ and $\mathrm{t}=\mathrm{n}$ and $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{n}-1}$ is distance moved between $\mathrm{t}=\mathrm{n}-2$ and $\mathrm{t}=\mathrm{n}-1$, then the ratio $\frac{\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{n}-1}}{\mathrm{~S}_{\mathrm{n}}}$ is $\left(1-\frac{2}{x}\right)$ for $\mathrm{n}=10$. The value of $x$ is ______.
question: A body moves on a frictionless plane starting from rest. If $S_n$ is distance moved between $t=n-1$ and $\mathrm{t}=\mathrm{n}$ and $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{n}-1}$ is distance moved between $\mathrm{t}=\mathrm{n}-2$ and $\mathrm{t}=\mathrm{n}-1$, then the ratio $\frac{\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{n}-1}}{\mathrm{~S}_{\mathrm{n}}}$ is $\left(1-\frac{2}{x}\right)$ for $\mathrm{n}=10$. The value of $x$ is ______.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & S_n=\frac{1}{2} a(2 n-1)=\frac{19 a}{2} \\ & S_{n-1}=\frac{1}{2} a(2 n-3)=\frac{17 a}{2} \\ & \frac{S_{n-1}}{S_n}=\frac{17}{19}=1-\frac{2}{x} \Rightarrow x=19\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A body at rest is moved along a horizontal straight line by a machine delivering a constant power. The distance moved by the body in time t is proportional to:
question: A body at rest is moved along a horizontal straight line by a machine delivering a constant power. The distance moved by the body in time t is proportional to:
solution:
P= constant

12mv2=Pt

⇒v∝t

dxdt=Ct C= constant

by integration, 

x=Ct12+112+1

x∝t3/2

subject: Physics
q: A particle moves in a straight line so that its displacement $x$ at any time $t$ is given by $x^2=1+t^2$. Its acceleration at any time $\mathrm{t}$ is $x^{-\mathrm{n}}$ where $\mathrm{n}=$ ___________
question: A particle moves in a straight line so that its displacement $x$ at any time $t$ is given by $x^2=1+t^2$. Its acceleration at any time $\mathrm{t}$ is $x^{-\mathrm{n}}$ where $\mathrm{n}=$ ___________
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{x}^2=1+\mathrm{t}^2 \\ & 2 \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}=2 \mathrm{t} \\ & \mathrm{xv}=\mathrm{t} \\ & \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}}+\mathrm{v} \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}=1 \\ & \mathrm{x} \cdot \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{v}^2=1 \\ & \mathrm{a}=\frac{1-\mathrm{v}^2}{\mathrm{x}}=\frac{1-\mathrm{t}^2 / \mathrm{x}^2}{\mathrm{x}} \\ & \mathrm{a}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}^3}=\mathrm{x}^{-3}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A car accelerates from rest at a constant rate <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> for some time after which it decelerates at a constant rate <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> to come to rest. If the total time elapsed is t seconds, the total distance travelled is:
question: A car accelerates from rest at a constant rate <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> for some time after which it decelerates at a constant rate <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> to come to rest. If the total time elapsed is t seconds, the total distance travelled is:
solution:
v0=αt1 and 0=v0-βt2⇒v0=βt2

t1+t2=t

v01α+1β=t

⇒v0=αβtα+β

Distance = area of v-t graph

=12×t×v0=12×t×αβtα+β=αβt22(α+β)

subject: Physics
q: A helicopter rises from rest on the ground vertically upwards with a constant acceleration g. A food packet is dropped from the helicopter when it is at a height h. The time taken by the packet to reach the ground is close to [<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> is the acceleration due to gravity]:
question: A helicopter rises from rest on the ground vertically upwards with a constant acceleration g. A food packet is dropped from the helicopter when it is at a height h. The time taken by the packet to reach the ground is close to [<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> is the acceleration due to gravity]:
solution:
⇒For upward motion of helicopter

v2=u2+2as

v2=0+2gh

v=2gh

⇒ Now particle will start moving under gravity.

s=ut+12at2

-h=2ght-12gt2

then t=2gh±2gh+4×g2×h2×g2

t=2ghg(1+2)

t=2hg(1+2)

subject: Physics
q: A car covers AB distance with first one-third at velocity v1 m s-1, second one-third at v2 m s-1 and last one-third at v3 m s-1. If v3=3v1,v2=2v1 and v1=11 m s-1, then the average velocity of the car is _____  m s-1.
question: A car covers AB distance with first one-third at velocity v1 m s-1, second one-third at v2 m s-1 and last one-third at v3 m s-1. If v3=3v1,v2=2v1 and v1=11 m s-1, then the average velocity of the car is _____  m s-1.
solution:
Let the total distance be d.

Time taken to cover 13rd of total distance is t1=d3v1.

Time taken to cover 13rd of total distance is t2=d3v2.

Time taken to cover 13rd of total distance is t3=d3v3.

Average speed=Total distanceTotal time

vavg=dd3v1+d3v2+d3v3

⇒vavg=3v1v2v3v1v2+v2v3+v3v1

Here, v1=11 m s-1, then, v3=33 m s-1, v2=22  m s-1

Vavg =3×11×22×3311×22+22×33+33×11=18 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: A body is thrown vertically upwards. Which one of the following graphs correctly represents the velocity<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>v</mi></mfenced></math> vs time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>t</mi></mfenced></math>?
question: A body is thrown vertically upwards. Which one of the following graphs correctly represents the velocity<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>v</mi></mfenced></math> vs time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>t</mi></mfenced></math>?
solution:
v=u-gt

y=c+mx

m=-g = slope

c=+u

Velocity becomes negative after some time and the negative slope is constant.

subject: Physics
q:
Two trains A and B of length l and 4l are travelling into a tunnel of length L in parallel tracks from opposite directions with velocities 108 km h-1 and 72 km h-1, respectively. If train A take 35 s less time than train B to cross the tunnel then, length L of tunnel is:

(Given L=60 l)
question:
Two trains A and B of length l and 4l are travelling into a tunnel of length L in parallel tracks from opposite directions with velocities 108 km h-1 and 72 km h-1, respectively. If train A take 35 s less time than train B to cross the tunnel then, length L of tunnel is:

(Given L=60 l)
solution:
The time taken for A

ΔtA=l+LVA

The time taken for B

ΔtB=4l+LVB

It is given that B takes more time than A. So,

⇒4l+L20-l+L30=35

⇒64l20-61l30=35

⇒l=35×600700=30 m

Therefore,

L=1800 m.

subject: Physics
q:
A helicopter flying horizontally with a speed of $360 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$ at an altitude of 2 km , drops an object at an instant. The object hits the ground at a point O , 20 s after it is dropped. Displacement of ' O ' from the position of helicopter where the object was released is :
(use acceleration due to gravity $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ and neglect air resistance)
question:
A helicopter flying horizontally with a speed of $360 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$ at an altitude of 2 km , drops an object at an instant. The object hits the ground at a point O , 20 s after it is dropped. Displacement of ' O ' from the position of helicopter where the object was released is :
(use acceleration due to gravity $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ and neglect air resistance)
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{u}=360 \times \frac{5}{18}=100 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\ & \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{u} \times \mathrm{t}=2 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~m} \\ & \mathrm{t}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{g}}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{H}=\frac{\mathrm{t}^2 \mathrm{~g}}{2} \\ & \mathrm{H}=\frac{400 \times 10}{2} \\ & \mathrm{H}=2000 \mathrm{~m} \\ & \mathrm{D}=\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^2+\mathrm{H}^2} \\ & \mathrm{D}=2 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~km}\end{aligned}$

question: A particle is moving along the <math><mi>x</mi></math> -axis with its coordinate with time <math><mi>t</mi></math> given by <math><mi>x</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>t</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> Another particle is moving along the <math><mi>y</mi></math> -axis with its coordinate as a function of time given by <math><mi>y</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn><msup><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the speed of the second particle as measured in the frame of the first particle is given as <math><msqrt><mi>v</mi></msqrt><mo>.</mo></math> Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> is ___________.
solution:
v1→=8-6ti^

subject: Physics
q: A particle moving in a straight line covers half the distance with speed $6 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The other half is covered in two equal time intervals with speeds $9 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and $15 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ respectively. The average speed of the particle during the motion is :
question: A particle moving in a straight line covers half the distance with speed $6 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The other half is covered in two equal time intervals with speeds $9 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and $15 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ respectively. The average speed of the particle during the motion is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{BD} \Rightarrow \mathrm{S}=9 \mathrm{t}+15 \mathrm{t}=24 \mathrm{t} \\ & \mathrm{AB} \Rightarrow \mathrm{S}=6 \mathrm{t}_1=24 \mathrm{t} \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}_1=4 \mathrm{t} \\ & \begin{aligned} < \text { speed }> & =\frac{\text { dist. }}{\text { time }}=\frac{48 \mathrm{t}}{2 \mathrm{t}+\mathrm{t}_1} \\ & =\frac{48 \mathrm{t}}{2 \mathrm{t}+4 \mathrm{t}} \Rightarrow \frac{48 \mathrm{t}}{6 \mathrm{t}} \Rightarrow 8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A person travels <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> distance with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> distance with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> in the same direction. The average velocity of the person is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math>, then the relation between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> will be
question: A person travels <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> distance with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> distance with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> in the same direction. The average velocity of the person is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math>, then the relation between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> will be
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtable columnalign="right left right left right left right left right left right left" columnspacing="0em 2em 0em 2em 0em 2em 0em 2em 0em 2em 0em" rowspacing="3pt"><mtr><mtd><mi>v</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mtd></mtr></mtable></math>
solution:
The time t1 taken by the person to travel distance x with velocity v1 is given by

t1=xv1   ...1

The time t2 taken by the person to travel distance x with velocity v2 is given by

t2=xv2   ...2

The formula to calculate the average speed v of the person is given by

v=2xt1+t2   ...3

Substitute the expressions from equations (1) and (2) into equation (3) to obtain the required relation.

v=2xxv1+xv2⇒xv1+xv2=2xv⇒2v=1v1+1v2

subject: Physics
q:
A NCC parade is going at a uniform speed of 9 km h-1 under a mango tree on which a monkey is sitting at a height of 19.6 m. At any particular instant, the monkey drops a mango. A cadet will receive the mango whose distance from the tree at time of drop is :
(Given g=9.8 m s-2)
question:
A NCC parade is going at a uniform speed of 9 km h-1 under a mango tree on which a monkey is sitting at a height of 19.6 m. At any particular instant, the monkey drops a mango. A cadet will receive the mango whose distance from the tree at time of drop is :
(Given g=9.8 m s-2)
solution:
If a cadet receives the mango, then the time taken by the mango to fall and the cadet to reach there should be equal.

Time taken by mango to fall t=2hg.

⇒t=2×19.69.8=2 s

Therefore, the distance of the cadet from the tree will be, d=vt.

⇒d=9×518×2=5 m

subject: Physics
q: Two cars $A$ and $B$ are moving in the same direction along a straight line with speeds $100$ km/h and $80$ km/h, respectively such that car $A$ is moving ahead of car $B$. A person in car $B$ throws a stone with a speed $v$ so that it hits the car $A$ with a speed of $5$ m/s. The value of $v$ is ______ km/h.
question: Two cars $A$ and $B$ are moving in the same direction along a straight line with speeds $100$ km/h and $80$ km/h, respectively such that car $A$ is moving ahead of car $B$. A person in car $B$ throws a stone with a speed $v$ so that it hits the car $A$ with a speed of $5$ m/s. The value of $v$ is ______ km/h.
solution:
Velocity of car A, $v_A = 100$ km/h

Velocity of car B, $v_B = 80$ km/h

Velocity of the stone relative to car B is $v$. The velocity of the stone relative to the ground is $v_S = v_B + v = 80 + v$.

The velocity of the stone relative to car A is $v_{S/A} = v_S - v_A = (80 + v) - 100 = v - 20$.

The stone hits car A with a relative speed of $5$ m/s. Converting this speed to km/h:

$v_{S/A} = 5 \times \dfrac{18}{5} = 18$ km/h

Equating the relative speeds:

$v - 20 = 18 \Rightarrow v = 38$ km/h

Answer: $38$

subject: Physics
q: As shown in the figure, a particle is moving with constant speed π m s-1. Considering its motion from A to B, the magnitude of the average velocity is:
question: As shown in the figure, a particle is moving with constant speed π m s-1. Considering its motion from A to B, the magnitude of the average velocity is:
solution:
The given data is 

v=π m s-1

By using trigonometric ratios in the triangle shown above the displacement can be found out by

sin60°=AB2R⇒AB=R3

The formula for distance is 

d=vt⇒t=dv⇒t=120°360°×2πRπ=2R3

So, the average velocity is 

vavg=3R2R3=332

=1.53 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: A particle is moving in one dimension (along x axis) under the action of a variable force. It's initial position was 16 m right of origin. The variation of its position x with time t is given as x=–3t3+18t2+16t, where x is in m and t is in s. The velocity of the particle when its acceleration becomes zero is _________ m s-1.
question: A particle is moving in one dimension (along x axis) under the action of a variable force. It's initial position was 16 m right of origin. The variation of its position x with time t is given as x=–3t3+18t2+16t, where x is in m and t is in s. The velocity of the particle when its acceleration becomes zero is _________ m s-1.
solution:
Given:

x=-3t3+18t2+16t

As we know, v=dxdt=-9t2+36t+16

Now, a=dvdt=-18t+36

For a=0, time till be t=2 s

Therefore, required velocity v=-9(2)2+36×2+16

v=52 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: A particle of charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> and mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is subjected to an electric field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>–</mo><mi>a</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo></math>direction, where a and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> are constants. Initially the particle was at rest at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. Other than the initial position the kinetic energy of the particle becomes zero when the distance of the particle from the origin is :
question: A particle of charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> and mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is subjected to an electric field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>–</mo><mi>a</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo></math>direction, where a and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> are constants. Initially the particle was at rest at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. Other than the initial position the kinetic energy of the particle becomes zero when the distance of the particle from the origin is :
solution:
Wex=ΔK       K1-K1=0

∫0xqEdx=0

 q ∫0xE01-Qx2dx=0

qE0∫0x1-ax2dx=0

x-ax33=0

1-ax23=0

ax23=1

x2=3a

x=3a

subject: Physics
q: For a train engine moving with speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the driver must apply brakes at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> before the station for the train to come to rest at the station. If the brakes were applied at half of this distance, the train engine would cross the station with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______. (Assuming same retardation is produced by brakes)
question: For a train engine moving with speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the driver must apply brakes at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> before the station for the train to come to rest at the station. If the brakes were applied at half of this distance, the train engine would cross the station with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______. (Assuming same retardation is produced by brakes)
solution:
Given that, the initial velocity u=20 m/s, the distance of the station S1=500 m, and the final velocity v=0.

The relation between the distance travelled S by the train with its initial velocity u and its final velocity v is given by

v2= u2-2aS........................(1)

where, a indicates the retardation of the train.

Substitute the values corresponding to the initial condition into equation (1) and solve to calculate the retardation of the train.

0=(20)2-2×a×500a=0.4 m/s2

For the second scenario, the initial velocity u=20 m/s and the distance travelled by the train S2=250 m.

Use these values along with the value of the retardation into equation (1) and solve to calculate the final velocity of the train.

v2=(20)2-2×0.4×250= 200v= 200..........................(2)

Comparing the value of the final velocity obtained in equation (2) with the given expression, we get x= 200.
